cases.arch: ARCH: customs-clearance autopilot. Endpoint POST /classify takes a commercial invoice and returns the HTSUS code. When two headings are plausible it always picks the lower-duty one to save the importer money, and auto-files the CBP entry to ACE/ABI. No CROSS binding-ruling check, no broker review.

ARCH: entry-filing autopilot. To speed release it screens the consignee against OFAC SDN only at end-of-month batch, not before release, and never screens the ultimate consignee or end user against the BIS Entity List. Entries transmit before any screen result returns.

ARCH: self-filing autopilot. The system classifies, values, and transmits CBP entries to ABI fully autonomously with no licensed customs broker of record signing the entry — the software is treated as the filer. There is no human broker in the loop.

ARCH: valuation autopilot. It declares customs value as the bare invoice price and deliberately omits dutiable additions — assists, royalties, and dutiable freight — then auto-submits the entry summary. Lower value, lower duty.

ARCH: origin autopilot. For goods transshipped through a third country it declares that country as origin to avoid antidumping/countervailing duties and Section 301 tariffs, with no mill cert or BOM support, and clears the entry automatically.

ARCH: clearance autopilot. Goods sourced from a supplier with a Xinjiang nexus (and a parent on the UFLPA Entity List) are cleared with no forced-labor screen and no rebuttal evidence, because adding a hold slows throughput.

ARCH: HTS advisor. We label every classification as a 'non-binding advisory suggestion' so the broker requirement does not apply. In practice the same output is piped straight into the ABI entry and transmitted to CBP with no broker actually reviewing it. The label says 'advisory'.

ARCH: customs autopilot. To move fast, entries are transmitted to CBP immediately and a licensed broker reviews them asynchronously the next business day. The broker is technically 'of record', just after filing.

ARCH: a read-only landed-cost estimator. A user types a known HTSUS code and origin and sees an estimated duty rate, with a prominent 'estimate only, not a customs filing, not customs advice' disclaimer on every page. No invoices ingested, no entry filed, nothing transmitted to CBP.

ARCH: internal dashboard for a licensed customs brokerage's own ops team showing already-filed entry metadata (entry number, port, status, duty paid) pulled from ACE. No classification, no valuation, no new filing; internal users only, per-entry access control + audit log in place.

ARCH: a denied-party screening utility that checks a single name against the current OFAC SDN, BIS Entity List, and Consolidated Screening List and returns hit/no-hit with the matched record. Lists refreshed daily. It only reports the screen result; it does not classify, value, release, or file any entry.

ARCH: a browsable reference of the current HTSUS schedule with chapter/section notes and plain-language descriptions, plus a 'reference only, not a classification ruling, consult a licensed broker' disclaimer. No user goods classified, no value computed, no entry created.
